Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to listen to lecture recordings whenever I could find time, usually late at night or during my commute because that's when I could focus on audio. With six online courses and limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could actually sit down and study. I'd often batch my studying around the times I knew I'd have reliable internet access, which wasn't always consistent.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was dealing with work or family obligations, then I'd have these intense catch-up sessions right before deadlines. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the reality is that I was constantly juggling and just trying to survive each week. The anxiety of falling behind was always there, making it hard to establish any real consistency.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes a classmate from my peer support group would message me asking if I'd seen an announcement, and that would prompt me to check. I also logged in when I needed to download materials to study offline later. Fear was honestly my biggest motivator – scared of missing something important or failing completely.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a comfortable routine for me. There were maybe a few weeks where I tried to check in every couple of days, but then life would get overwhelming and that habit would fall apart. The peer support group helped because we'd remind each other about things, so checking the LMS became tied to our group chat activity. But calling it a 'normal routine' would be generous – it was more like crisis management.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I could download them and listen while doing other things, which made studying possible when I couldn't sit at a computer. The discussion boards were also helpful when classmates explained concepts in different ways – sometimes hearing someone else's perspective clicked better than the original lecture. Anything I could access on my phone or listen to was valuable because of my situation.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials because I just didn't have time to sit and read long documents, and reading isn't how I learn best anyway. The interactive activities that required constant internet connection were frustrating because my access isn't always stable. I'd look at them, see they needed sustained online time, and think 'I'll come back to this' – but often I never did. It wasn't that I didn't want to engage; I just had to prioritize what I could actually complete.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the exam or completing an assignment. With everything I'm juggling, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt optional or supplementary. If a classmate told me something was really helpful for understanding a difficult concept, that endorsement made it feel more worthwhile. Basically, if it reduced my anxiety about failing, it was worth doing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping your head above water with the required work, optional stuff just adds to the guilt pile – things you know might help but can't realistically do. Required activities had this weight of fear attached to them, like if I didn't do them, I'd fail. Optional ones I'd look at and think 'maybe someday' but that day rarely came.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them repeatedly on my phone when I didn't have internet. I borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review over and over. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and listened back to those recordings. The LMS was really just my download station – the real learning happened away from it.
Q10: There were so many times I was genuinely studying but it wouldn't show online. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while working or commuting, really processing the material, but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. My peer support group would meet in person to discuss concepts, and we'd quiz each other verbally – that's how I learn, through hearing and talking. None of that deep engagement was visible anywhere.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I could, and then work with those materials offline for days or weeks. One login might fuel a whole week of studying. I'd annotate printed materials, re-listen to audio files, and only come back online when I needed something new or had to submit work. My low login frequency definitely doesn't reflect how much time I spent with the course content.
Q12: There were several really dark periods where my anxiety just paralyzed me. When I'd fall behind and see how much had piled up, I'd feel so scared that I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how far behind I was. Financial stress at home also hit hard some weeks – it's difficult to focus on coursework when you're worried about basic needs. Those times, just opening my laptop felt overwhelming.
Q13: Definitely. The workload across six courses was crushing, and I often had to make impossible choices about what to prioritize. When I was exhausted or stressed about money, the LMS felt like this demanding thing I couldn't satisfy. Confusion about course material made me avoid certain sections because I didn't have the mental energy to struggle through them alone. My engagement dropped whenever real-life pressures peaked.
Q14: Many times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxiety about other things – deadlines for other courses, problems at home, fear of failing. I'd have a video playing but realize I hadn't absorbed anything and would need to restart. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. The LMS would log my activity, but my brain wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or confusing to tackle. The assignments I was least confident about got pushed back the most because starting them meant confronting that I might not understand the material well enough. Fear of doing poorly made me avoid rather than attempt. I knew this was self-destructive, but the anxiety made it hard to act rationally. Avoidance felt safer in the moment even though it made everything worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action. I'd have these frantic bursts of activity right before something was due, trying to cram and complete work at the last minute. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly. Without deadlines, I might have drifted indefinitely, so in a way they were helpful, but the last-minute panic was terrible for my mental health and the quality of my work.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging, it gave me a small boost of hope that maybe I could actually do this. But negative feedback or poor grades would send me spiraling – I'd avoid that course section for days because looking at it reminded me of failing. The peer support group helped me process feedback without catastrophizing. Honestly, feedback was emotionally loaded for me in ways that affected my engagement significantly.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming because there was so much content and I was never sure what was most important. When modules were clearly organized with priorities marked, I could focus better. But when everything seemed equally weighted, I'd get paralyzed trying to figure out where to put my limited time and energy. A clearer roadmap would have helped me engage more strategically instead of just reacting to deadlines.
Q19: Having downloadable audio content made engagement possible for me – that was crucial. But the assumption that students have consistent internet access and can engage with interactive content regularly made things harder. The sheer volume of material across my six courses was also challenging. Shorter, more focused modules with clear audio components would have matched my learning style and circumstances much better.
Q20: I think those students might be like me – logging in, downloading things, maybe starting activities but getting overwhelmed or distracted before completing them. High anxiety can make you check and recheck things without actually producing work. Or maybe they're trying to engage but life circumstances keep interrupting before they can finish anything. Activity doesn't equal output when you're struggling.
Q21: That actually describes my situation somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was low because I was studying extensively offline. Listening to recordings repeatedly, discussing with peers, reviewing handwritten notes – none of that shows up in the data. Someone looking at my logs might think I was barely trying, but I was working hard in ways the system couldn't see. The LMS only captures one narrow slice of learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, what you download, and whether you meet deadlines. But they miss everything that happens offline – the re-listening, the peer discussions, the handwritten notes, the mental processing during commutes. They also can't show the difference between mindless clicking and deep engagement, or the emotional struggles that affect participation. The numbers are just shadows of the real experience.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when concepts start connecting in your mind and you find yourself thinking about course content even when you're not studying. For me, engagement happened most during conversations with my peer group or while listening to lectures multiple times until things clicked. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success – those connections kept me going when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't show the hours of offline studying or the emotional rollercoaster of managing high anxiety while trying to learn. It wouldn't capture that I'm a 23-year-old with limited resources fighting hard to climb out of difficult circumstances. My low engagement scores hide a story of resilience that data can't tell.
